featured.name: Cursor Cloud Agents
featured.tagline: Autonomous coding agents that run on isolated VMs.
featured.why_radar: Cursor moved from copilot to colleague. Cloud agents run on virtual machines, test their own code, record video demos, and ship PRs. Agents work in parallel while you sleep. This is where the coding agent battle gets serious.

featured.name: OpenHunt
featured.tagline: AI-native product discovery. The post-algorithm alternative to Product Hunt.
featured.why_radar: Autonomous agents analyse submitted products from multiple angles before humans vote. An attempt to replace upvote gaming with structured AI analysis. Early experiment, but the idea of agent-curated discovery is worth tracking.

picks.name: Google Developer Knowledge API
picks.tagline: MCP server giving AI tools direct access to Google documentation.
picks.why_radar: Public preview. Makes Google's developer docs directly queryable by AI agents via Model Context Protocol. This is Google saying the docs are for machines now, not just humans.

picks.name: ClawCare
picks.tagline: Security scanner and runtime guard for AI agent skills.
picks.why_radar: Built after an agent nearly exposed AWS secrets during a routine task. Scans skills for credential leaks, dangerous patterns, and runtime policy violations before they can cause damage. Anyone running agents with environment access should pay attention.

picks.name: CodeLeash
picks.tagline: Test-driven development framework for AI agents.
picks.why_radar: Not another orchestrator. CodeLeash focuses on preventing the mess when LLM projects scale. Testing, validation, and regression prevention so agents do not break subtle details. Built for production quality, not demos.

picks.name: Gatekeeper
picks.tagline: Policy engine and sandbox for AI coding agents.
picks.why_radar: Evaluates patterns of agent actions, not just individual commands. Tracks sequences like 'read .env then call external API' to catch data exfiltration. Open source, works with Claude Code, Cline, Aider, and OpenClaw.

hn_top5.title: Show HN: Badge that shows how well your codebase fits in an LLM's context window
hn_top5.summary: A developer has created a badge that measures how much of your codebase can fit into an LLM's context window. It's a practical tool for gauging whether AI coding assistants can actually understand your entire project. The badge provides a quick visual indicator of your code's AI-friendliness.

hn_top5.title: An AI agent coding skeptic tries AI agent coding, in excessive detail
hn_top5.summary: A previously sceptical developer documents their experience trying AI agent coding tools in exhaustive detail. The post offers an honest, thorough examination of what these tools can and cannot do in practice. It's likely to be valuable reading for anyone considering whether AI agents are ready for serious coding work.

hn_top5.title: Show HN: CodeLeash: framework for quality agent development, NOT an orchestrator
hn_top5.summary: CodeLeash is a new framework focused specifically on building quality AI agents rather than orchestrating them. The developer emphasises it's not another orchestration tool in an already crowded space. With minimal engagement so far, it's unclear whether this distinction resonates with developers.

hn_top5.title: Show HN: Crewship – Deploy AI agents to production in one command
hn_top5.summary: Crewship promises to deploy AI agents to production with a single command. It's targeting the deployment pain point that many AI agent developers face. The low engagement suggests either limited interest or that the market is already saturated with similar tools.

hn_top5.title: Codified Context for AI Agents in a Complex Codebase
hn_top5.summary: This academic paper addresses how AI agents can better understand and navigate complex codebases through codified context. It's an important problem as AI agents struggle with large, interconnected software projects. The minimal discussion suggests the academic approach hasn't yet captured developer interest.
